text:
MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 64-year-old female with known Stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant lung adenocarcinoma. Prior definitive chemoradiation followed by osimertinib maintenance. Disease progression approximately 14 months after osimertinib initiation with new systemic lesions and two intracranial metastases -- right frontal lobe (1.4 cm) and left cerebellar hemisphere (0.7 cm) -- treated with stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) approximately 3 months ago. Currently receiving second-line systemic carboplatin/pemetrexed chemotherapy. This study obtained for routine post-SRS intracranial surveillance. Prior intracranial MRI for comparison.

TECHNIQUE: Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the brain was performed on a 3.0 Tesla MRI system. Sequences obtained include sagittal T1, axial T2/FLAIR, axial and coronal T1 post-gadolinium (gadoteridol, 0.1 mmol/kg IV), axial DWI with ADC mapping, axial GRE/susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI), and axial T1 MPRAGE post-contrast. High-resolution volumetric post-contrast images were obtained and multiplanar reconstructions reviewed. Patient received standard gadolinium-based contrast without complication. Comparison: Prior MRI brain with and without contrast obtained at the time of confirmed progression (approximately 3 months prior).

FINDINGS:

Prior SRS Sites:

Right Frontal Lobe: The previously identified right frontal lobe metastasis treated with SRS (20 Gy, single fraction) has substantially decreased in overall size and signal complexity. The lesion now measures approximately 0.9 cm in greatest dimension on the current study (previously 1.4 cm at the time of progression imaging). The lesion demonstrates a thin rim of peripheral enhancement on post-gadolinium sequences with a relatively hypointense central core on T1 and heterogeneous signal on T2/FLAIR, a pattern most consistent with evolving post-treatment effect and central coagulative necrosis rather than viable tumor recurrence. The surrounding T2/FLAIR signal abnormality representing vasogenic edema has markedly diminished compared with the prior study -- previously up to approximately 2.8 cm of surrounding edema signal; now reduced to a narrow halo of perilesional T2 signal, likely representing residual gliosis. No restricted diffusion within the lesion core to suggest acute ischemia. On SWI, scattered susceptibility artifact within the lesion is noted, consistent with blood products in the context of radiation necrosis. No enlarging enhancing nodule or irregular wall thickening to raise concern for viable intracranial tumor at this site at present.

Left Cerebellar Hemisphere: The previously documented left cerebellar metastasis (0.7 cm, treated with SRS, 22 Gy single fraction) is no longer identifiable on the current study. There is no discrete enhancing lesion, no T2/FLAIR signal abnormality, and no restricted diffusion in the left cerebellum at or adjacent to the prior treatment site. A small focus of susceptibility artifact on SWI may represent a hemosiderin deposit at the treatment site, which is a recognized post-SRS finding. The remainder of the cerebellar parenchyma appears symmetric and normal in signal. This represents a complete radiographic response at this treatment site.

Other Intracranial Findings:

Extra-axial Lesion: The small extra-axial lesion at the right parietal convexity noted on multiple prior studies remains stable in size, measuring approximately 4 mm. This lesion demonstrates homogeneous enhancement with a dural attachment on post-contrast sequences and does not display restricted diffusion or perilesional edema, morphologically most consistent with a small meningioma as previously characterized. No interval growth. No suspicious features.

Cerebral Parenchyma: No new enhancing lesions are identified in the supratentorial or infratentorial compartments. The cerebral cortex, white matter, basal ganglia, thalami, brainstem, and cerebellum appear within normal limits for patient age. No areas of restricted diffusion to suggest acute infarction. No abnormal parenchymal signal to raise concern for new metastatic deposits on any sequence reviewed.

Ventricles and CSF Spaces: The ventricular system is normal in caliber and configuration. The sulci and cisterns are patent and appropriate for age. No hydrocephalus. No midline shift.

Leptomeningeal Enhancement: No abnormal leptomeningeal or pachymeningeal enhancement identified. The cranial nerves, dural sinuses, and skull base appear unremarkable.

Calvarium and Scalp: No osseous lesions of concern identified.

IMPRESSION:

1. Excellent intracranial response to stereotactic radiosurgery. The right frontal treated metastasis has decreased substantially in size (from 1.4 cm to approximately 0.9 cm) with central necrosis, markedly reduced peripheral edema, and rim enhancement pattern most consistent with post-treatment/radiation necrosis rather than active residual disease. No unequivocal imaging features of progression at this site on the current study.

2. Complete radiographic response at the left cerebellar SRS site. The previously documented left cerebellar metastasis is no longer visible, consistent with complete treatment response.

3. No new intracranial metastatic lesions identified.

4. No leptomeningeal disease.

5. Stable 4 mm right parietal extra-axial lesion, consistent with known meningioma, unchanged.

Radiologist: [SIGNATURE REDACTED]
Dictated and electronically signed
